text.title: Heuristic Evaluation Examples and Antipatterns
text.summary: Example findings with full location/heuristic/severity/recommendation structure, severity rating examples, and antipatterns in evaluation practice.

example.note: Well-formed finding — checkout page, heuristic #5, severity 3
example.code: Location: Checkout page → Payment form → Card number field Heuristic: #5 Error Prevention Problem: No validation for credit card number format. Users can enter letters and invalid numbers without warning until form submission — causing a full-page error after they have entered all other fields. Severity: 3 (Major) Recommendation: Add real-time format validation. Show checkmarks as user enters valid card number sections. Auto-detect card type from first 4 digits.

Location: Settings → Account → Delete Account Heuristic: #3 User Control and Freedom Problem: "Delete Account" button immediately deletes without confirmation. Action is irreversible. No undo. Severity: 4 (Catastrophic) Recommendation: Add confirmation dialog requiring the user to type their email address to confirm deletion. Show consequences ("Your data will be permanently deleted in 30 days") with a cancel window.

list.item: 0: "The icon on the settings page is slightly outdated but universally recognized" — preference, not a usability issue
1: "Help link in footer uses the same color as body text, making it less visible" — cosmetic, not task-blocking
2: "Help documentation link is buried 3 menu levels deep" — minor, users can find it with effort
3: "Cannot complete checkout without discovering the non-obvious 'Continue' button" — major, causes significant failure rate
4: "Critical function 'Submit Order' is broken on mobile Safari" — catastrophic, blocks core task

antipattern.description: Evaluators collaborating or discussing before individual evaluations are complete
antipattern.reason: Early discussion anchors all evaluators to the first person's findings. Later evaluators fail to explore areas already covered and underweight areas not mentioned. Independent evaluation is the core quality mechanism of the method.

antipattern.description: Vague findings: "The UI is confusing" or "Navigation is hard to use"
antipattern.reason: Cannot be reproduced, prioritized, or actioned. Minimum valid finding: location + heuristic + specific problem + severity + recommendation. All five fields are required.

antipattern.description: Single evaluator evaluation presented as comprehensive
antipattern.reason: One evaluator finds ~35% of issues. Claiming comprehensive coverage from a single-evaluator review sets false expectations. Always note evaluator count in the report methodology section.

antipattern.description: Agent declaring "no violations found" for a heuristic without explicit reasoning
antipattern.reason: Either the interface is genuinely excellent for that heuristic, or the evaluator did not look thoroughly. A "no violations" declaration requires a brief explanation of why the heuristic is satisfied, not just absence of findings.

rule.statement: Use heuristic evaluation to find expert-detectable violations before usability testing. Use usability testing to find real-user struggles that only emerge from domain-specific tasks and actual usage behavior.
rule.rationale: The two methods are complementary, not substitutes. Heuristic evaluation is faster and cheaper but finds violations, not user behavior. Usability testing with real users finds struggles that expert evaluators miss because they know the system too well. Run both for complete coverage.
